review_excerpt: On one project, he mass accused and penalized 15% of the class for cheating using compiler-generated code or AI because they used an "advanced" command, even though it was listed in the class slides, project rubric, project description, textbook, and an approved linked webpage on his course site.
He also said we could reference compiler-generated code for the project and even bring AI-generated notes and content to exams, which makes it really unclear about what’s actually allowed.
He originally accused even more students for the same issue until realizing he had accidentally included a non-project-related file in his checker.

He said he had high confidence in the accusation, and offered a 0 to those accused on the project (~2% overall grade reduction) or a one-on-one interview process where success would lead to a regrade request and failure results in a XF recommendation to OSC.

Either way, Kauffman later wrote in a post that he "was never once convinced of a student's innocence during an interview" and those who were let go only had "plausible deniability." The process was extremely stressful and lacked transparency.

His passive aggressive comments about not giving extra credit on later projects because of the cheating cases and verbal/written rants about "cheating" students devaluing the CS degree before any of the cases were even resolved made the class feel toxic, distrustful, and stressful like everyone was constantly under suspicion.

On top of the difficult content, the threat of being accused and kicked out of the major over one or two lines of code on a project that is flagged as suspicious could seriously impact anyone’s mental health.
All lectures are recorded, exams are graded fairly, labs encourage group work and actual learning.
Each topic is explained in great detail, there is a ton of resources provided outside of lecture to study and review.
The course itself is also quite fun, but can be challenging.
Also open note midterms and final is pretty great.
I barely scraped by and ended with a 73% which resulted in a C, even after always scoring below the average on all his exams and quizzes.
The way that the course was structured, at least when I took his class, was set up in a way that the projects and exercises made up a substantial part of the overall course grade.
Lab works also made up a small but considerable percentage that can boost your grade by the time finals come around.

If I am not mistaken at the beginning of the course, he acknowledged that his exams and quizzes were known to be difficult, but that as long as you completed the projects and exercises on time and tried your best in exams and quizzes you were going to be okay.
Projects: His projects were not inherently difficult, they were at times confusing, but attending office hours and posting on Piazza ultimately cleared any confusions you may have had.
Exercises could often be done in a day, but I would suggest not doing this for both exercises and projects.
Lab works were also easy to complete, and we were given enough time to complete them.

Just by completing these on time, you can be at a good standing before exams and quizzes tank you grade (and they will, unless you are the type to ruin the curve.) Exams/Quizzes: His exams and quizzes were confusing, tedious, and soul draining.
Often times it was never truly clear what exactly we were going to be tested on.

Before exams or quizzes, he would send out emails containing a list of what we had covered since the previous quiz/exam, and he would say that what was on the slides was testable material, so it would be hard to differentiate what exactly was going to be on the test/quiz.

Part of the shortcomings related to quizzes and exams *could* be attributed to a student's lack of complete familiarity and understanding of the material, however, unless the only thing you have going for you is school (and that is okay, great actually, if it's the case for you - it is not often, rarely actually, for that to be the case for everyone) then it is very difficult to develop mastery of the material in time before exams and quizzes.
Some topics may be covered a lecture or two before an exam/quiz and some of the questions on quizzes and exams feel like they are meant to be 'gotchas' or may be super specific to a covered topic.

For example, though the understanding of unix commands is useful in various industries, some questions often relied on a lab discussion slide that may have shown some [blank] unix command that was only used then and never really again - to some students those questions may have felt like free points, but when you are handling other courses and only have a vague idea of what was to be on a quiz then, some miscellaneous unix command would feel like the least of your concerns.
I personally scored poorly on exams and quizzes, at some point getting a 39% on an exam, fully tanking my grade and putting my chances to pass on the line.
However, because I submitted all other projects, exercises and labs on time, my grade was brought up just enough to pass.
If you come to his office hours or email him, he is very helpful and he often responds to emails fast.
Moreover, if you maintain a clear communication with him about certain issues and difficulties, he is often willing to help and possibly grant small extensions for submissions.
I would say, do not walk into his class seeing him as some sort of evil supervillain who sits in his office petting a cat while plotting on how to make his next exam as hard as possible.

If I am not mistaken, being a professor is not his main role at the university, take a look at his extensive resume and his achievements and you sort of realize that being a professor is just a role he has to play, and he can only have so much time on his hands, so balancing all of these responsibilities may be difficult even for him.
Attend all his lectures and you will realize that not many people show up.
Often times this gets confusing, as he draws on top of the slides, so it is not visually the best and easiest thing to understand unless you follow along closely.

If you walk into class during these moments when he draws out topics, it looks like that one picture 'my coworker losing his damn mind.' Moreover, sometimes he goes back and forth with a lot of coding examples and slides, so it gets a little confusing to understand.
It is hard to keep up sometimes and it would often require revisiting his recorded lectures to get a better grasp of a concept.
On the times he does do well, then it is easy to follow along and he does a good job at walking you through some of the topics.
This semester, we only found out about our exam results a couple of days before the final.
Some projects take a long time to get graded, and the late submission turns on after 11 pm of the on-time submission date.
Some lab works were graded the day of grade submissions, so it was difficult to truly get what your grade was throughout the semester.
Honestly, it was gut wrenching not knowing where you stood before the final exam was added to the grade book.
He would often curve, at some point in some semester he curved by 8% percent, but this semester he did not curve as much, only slightly so that was also gut wrenching.
His exams and quizzes are hard and may tank your grade, but the class is set up in a way where your grade is able to take it if you do all the other work on time.
The worst part about this class are the projects, they're long and hard, start early.
Tons of people get caught using GPT for the projects, but he's talked about somehow implementing it for future semesters to be more AI friendly/accommodating.

I will say, the TAs were very hit or miss this semester and any real complaints I do have about the course come down to that, since the grading can take a while and some of the responses on piazza were not the best.
His teaching style is to essentially overload you with a bunch of information, most of which isn't relevant for the upcoming task such as project or test/quizzes.
Yeah he'll make those small things important questions on his test/quizzes.
To give you an example, literally the night of our first exam, he assigned the next coding project and told us "I suggest you get started on this immediately".
I got the notification for the assignment literally late night while me and a friend were studying for HIS exam that was going to happen later that day in Mckeldin.
And he also gives little-to-no time between projects to breath.
Speaking of the projects, his project documents are longwinded and vague.
And once they finally helped me, it took them like 10 minutes to find the issue and relay it to me.
There has also been multiply times where TAs will change their hours without updating the google calendar, and as a commuter it's just rage inducing.
Like bro I'm wasting gas and time and he's so tight with his deadlines it makes no sense that he's chill about this.
So essentially you can turn in a assignment late only 24 hours after it's initial due date.
So if it wad due the 21st at 11pm (yes all his projects are due at 11 pm, not 11:59pm), then the late due date is the 22nd at 11pm.
And he will reduce the overall grade for this assignment by 15%.
But don't get this confused with the GFA requirements, where if you don't meet them for each project you will be dropped by a letter grade.
You can go back for any project to meet the GFA requirements (which could be to just pass all public test for example), but you won't get a new grade for that assignment.
So if you get a 75% on that project, that's what you're stuck with.
It's unnecessary stress and I'd recommend that you don't pair this class with cmsc250.

It's needless and truly from my experience, I won't be able to say with confidence that I know how to code C by the end of this class since I and many others just understand enough to get the projects out of our faces and to get barely passing grades on his quizzes and exams.

First exam took me from feeling like I'd get an A to feeling like "lets pray for a curve to a C-" even after doing great on the quizzes.

The only avg I can see for quizzes so far is quiz1, which is ~20/30 so right in line with what people say of "most people technically fail every assessment.", so take my own grade with a grain of short term salt.
I don't personally have any issue with the lectures but I also don't pay any attention during them, since skimming the slides is usually plenty, but that really doesn't excuse the teaching quality.
The projects aren't that bad, as long as you sit down and do them with a couple days of time to work since they can involve a lot of writing code even if its straightforward.

My only issue with the projects is the same issue I have with quizzes and assume I will have with the exams: the requirements (or on assessments the phrasing of questions) is usually so poor that they simply don't make sense or are missing key details for you to do the work.

It's been the case on every project so far that there is an absolute deluge of posts asking "what does this actually mean" about project specs, because the phrasing of a spec will be vague enough that you could interpret it many different ways and the public tests will be so incredibly simple that they don't address a single one of the clarifications needed.
There's also often not actually any details given on expected formatting for things that require formatting, and TAs/instructor will sometimes give conflicting answers with each other to questions.
Whether I end this class with a C- or an A+ I'll leave having learned the exact same amount from the lectures and projects: absolutely nothing.

I thought projects 1, 2, and 5 were relatively straight forward with a decent difficulty, project 3 was pretty difficult because Assembly is just like that, but project 4 was really hard because it was assigned late (giving us less time to do it), but it was an added difficulty to figure out which tests were actually passing because it would work half of the time due to the concurrency (sometimes, tests would pass on grace, but completely fail on gradescope).
The exams were actually not that bad, they tested content covered in the most recent project to the date of the exam, and were open note.

But I think the open note concept is kind of misleading, it doesn't make the exam easier, it just made it a different kind of test, covering applications of topics rather than fundamentals which you may be used to from previous courses.
The labs and hw were good practice if you diligently did them, and were overall pretty free grades.

Full breakdown below: Lectures: Fawzi is probably the best lecturer I’ve had at Maryland.
Fawzi has a way of breaking down concepts in a very simple, easy to follow, and entertaining way that makes his lectures really easy to follow and understand.
Exams: He did complain that he wrote exams that were consistently too easy and mentioned they were going to get harder, but they never got harder, so I’ll take it.
His exams consistently have pretty high scores, take mostly lighter content from the lecture, and are basically what you expect from going to lecture.
One thing I will say is he can sometimes grade things pretty harshly with regards to partial credit.
Quizzes: The pop quizzes are the biggest issue with this class for me.
They’re mainly a way of keeping people going to discussions, since any given discussion can have a quiz, so you have to go to all of them.
All the quizzes are pretty straightforward, and you should normally score 20/20 on them if you’re keeping up with the material.
Don’t be afraid to send a regrade request if your grade is messed up and you have an equivalent answer, but don’t send any begging for partial credit (the same applies to exams).
TL;DR: Fantastic lecturer, pretty simple exams, and nice person.

In terms of course structure, the projects are the most demanding part of this course.
Especially project 5 (you'll see it when you get there).
I know this is said in every CS course review ever, but I cannot emphasize this enough when I say that you should start these projects THE DAY they are assigned.

Now I will say there were some people who would start the projects with 2-3 days before due date (most people have other overlapping courses like 351) so they can be a bit constrained for time, but still would get it done somehow.
However, like I said, the projects are the most important source of learning in the course.
Use the projects to master the concepts you learn in class (I did NOT do this, hypocrite much??Shh keep reading) and you will see that the exams and quizzes are SO much easier.
Any sort of "coding" question you get in a quiz or exam is some permutation of a function you implemented for a relevant project.

But once you have enough practice with the first two projects (really only the first project because it is designed to teach you how functional programming in OCaml works), you will spend less time worrying about the language itself rather implementing the necessary concepts into the projects.

my main two complaints regarding the class were (1) i felt as if rust was not taught well because he only went over the theory of what makes rust different from other programming languages and the two rust projects were honestly free-for-alls, and (2) because of the content of the class changing frequently, studying from previous exams can only help so much.

The grading was 40% projects, so very generous in terms of exam weight.
The lecture quizzes, projects, discussion content, TAs, quizzes, and exam content are all exactly the same.

The only difference between Cliff and Anwar's sections are which professor's lecture you can attend, but even then, you can just not go to lecture or go to the other professor's lecture (some people found that they didn't really like Cliff's lectures and attended Anwar's instead---the class is big enough that I don't think people would notice).
Mamat and Cliff's sections): - All the class information is available here: https://bakalian.cs.umd.edu/330 - Literally 44% of your grade is free.
Projects make up 40% of your grade and you get around two weeks for each of them (give or take).
You can ask questions, ask the TAs, look up documentation, submit as many times as you want, etc.
Each project took me between two and eight hours.
There is literally no reason not to get 100% on every project as long as you don't procrastinate.
The median on every project was between 97% to 100%.
So again, there's literally no reason not to get 100% on every project, and they make up 40% of your grade.
- 4% of your grade is lecture quizzes, which are completely open internet.
You can run the code on your own machine, and the lowest couple of lecture quizzes get dropped.
So between the lecture quizzes and the projects, 44% of your grade should be completely free (as long as you don't procrastinate).
- The quiz medians were: Quiz 1: 14.5/20, Quiz 2: 15/20, Quiz 3: 17.25/20, Quiz 4: 16/20.
You get access to all of the course's quizzes and solutions from previous semesters, which is helpful for studying.
I honestly found the quizzes to be harder than the midterms.
I will say, though, that I didn't do very well on the first two quizzes, but after that, I started buckling down and really studying and I got 100s on the last two quizzes.
You'll be fine as long as you study and really understand the content.
- 46% of your grade is two midterms and the final.
The medians were: Midterm 1: 83, Midterm 2: 87.5, Final (with extra credit): 89.48.
Just like the quizzes, you get access to every previous midterm and their answer key, and around ~85% of the content on these exams is the same every semester.

They do change things up a bit sometimes (like I think we did more garbage collection than they did in previous semesters), but most of the exams cover very similar content to previous semesters, so it's really helpful to take previous exams for practice.
There were good review questions, but they were all in a publicly-available GitHub repository that you could look at without going to discussion.
For Cliff in particular, I thought his lectures are okay.
He can get pretty theoretical sometimes.
TLDR; This is a fairly easy class as long as you don't procrastinate on your projects and do previous quizzes/exams as practice.
I would recommend not starting the projects any later than the weekend before they're due.

c) Memorize, and then change up and iterate whatever practice exam material questions provided.
If you do these things, the exams are quite frankly not extremely hard.

As the last review says, this is also my first time writing a review (that says enough already).
This is by far the most disorganized, poorly structured, and poorly taught class I have ever taken by far.

Now for some perhaps, a structure where you learn from poorly recorded videos and irrelevant in-person lectures is just a blast, where you don't know midterm and final topics until 5 days before the exams.
Comparing this to taking CMSC330 simultaneously, this is a complete 180, where that class has plenty of practice material, notes and lecture recordings available, etc.

You have nothing to study for on exams, expect practically needing to memorize all the relevant recorded lectures, as well as having a chance at a problem or two appearing from a previous exam a few years back.
Note that all you are provided for study material, is a previous exam, and irrelevant practice questions that exist to get you to think critically, and that's about it.

Quizzes are due the morning of the in person lecture, and often aren't announced or posted until the evening of the day before, so god forbid you're studying or doing work for another class, you have no choice but to focus on that first.
They took forever to grade things, even simple multiple choice quizzes, not to mention not getting your exam feedback returned until the next exam comes around.
As for the professor, he is more likely than not on tenure, concerns have been voiced regarding his teaching millions of times, and nothing has been done.
You will not even get a GLANCE at what your grade is in the class, til grades are due (note that your final and other quizzes are still not graded, that's why I said a glance).
You will spend your whole semester biting your nails and ripping your hair out, not knowing what the cutoff will be like, how you're doing in the class, and what curveball will be thrown at you next.
It's important to add, that Kruskal grades on a curve, so when you see the class averaging 50 on exams, I wouldn't think too much of it, as I'll provide the cutoffs below.
I don't understand how this could be the best way to go about grading for this class.
Why not create manageable problems, curriculum, exams, etc.
Note that as far as I understand it, as long as you land within the standard deviation on exams, given your quiz and homework grades are good enough, you will pass.
Tips: -Memorize those lecture recordings like there's no tomorrow.
-Do the Homeworks yourself!
Exams will consist of one or two reworded homework problems.
-In-person lectures are mostly a waste of time, unless you're genuinely curious about the irrelevant information provided to you, so use that time to study instead.

-Your best practice material will be a previous year's exam and your homeworks, and those consist of 50 to 66% of the exam -For the "thinking" problems, try to get some practice online or from Leetcode, based on what the "thinking" problem is like from the previous exam -Only rely on all of the above if you were not able to get a spot in Justin's class, but I would do everything in my ability to go back and take his class instead.

Before I provide grading metrics, I want to summarize that this is a counter-intuitive, self-learning course, with an elderly hard of hearing professor, who doesn't plan on changing or modifying the structure of his course whatsoever.

I am by any means not a morning person, but I do consider myself to be organized, so if sacrificing those early hours of sleep and not losing my ability to sleep is available (along with receiving a better education), choose Justin, and do everything in your power to do so.

Last but not least, even with the tips provided above, you will still often encounter problems on exams that are nothing like what you studied for, and it's not uncommon to encounter a topic which lacks fundamental resources to even know what to expect from it.

None of this prepared me for when I lost over half the points on a problem from Homework 1 asking me to prove the Big Omega of a certain function.

Grade Breakdown: MCQs: 7.69% Homework: 15.38% Exams: 46.15% Final Exam: 30.78% Total: 100% Most of the MCQs were straightforward, with about 14 in total this semester.
You should be able to score 100% on most of them, though the number of MCQs and homework assignments may change each semester, as well as the number that are dropped.
Many students, including myself, used a group chat to compare answers, and we attended office hours for clarification on more open-ended questions.
While I didn’t go to Ting’s office hours, I’ve heard they’re helpful.
As for Ting's exams, there’s a good amount of partial credit, so even if you make a few mistakes, it’s rare that you’d need to request a regrade.
However, exams accounted for roughly 77% of our grade, so even if you perform well on homework and MCQs, you still need to do well on the midterms and final to get an A.
If you handle exam pressure well and avoid small mistakes adding up, you should be in a strong position for this class.

Week 1 - 3: (Intro stuff, basic set theory, combinations, counting (discrete math stuff), geometric probability, sigma algebra (wtf is that), conditional probability, and some Bayes theorem) Kinda bad, didn't have good examples through class, but wasn't the worst professor ever, started to record lectures with Notability, used the microphone (so ppl in the back of the room can hear him), and has PDFs of the notes after 2-3 weeks.

This is when he turned around and became the GOAT, went to his office hours a lot between exam 1 and 2 to ask for him to help us a bit with either doing practice problems, bonus points, practice, or (Telling us exactly what type of questions would be).
The Tuesday right before exam 2, he said, "You have 5 questions.
These are the topics to study up on, kiddos." It definitely helped me, which was nice.
There was like 1 problem on negative hypergeometric (we didn't learn it, but we had to try and derive it; most ppl got 13/20 on it was super lenient on grading).

Week (11 - 13): (Joint RV, Joint pdf, marginal pdf, conditional pdf, expected values, covariance and correlation, some intro statistic stuff, point estimation, methods of point estimation (Method of Moments) He started to do curves this week (thank the heavens).
The examples were at least starting to get better, and he kinda explained like why they mattered and what it kinda means in the bigger picture.
Some things to note about the other reviews for this class, most of them were made after the first midterm.
which was quite difficult and had a pretty low average.
After some discussing, Professor Zheng agreed to add a curve to all the exams and the final and it was a pretty nice curve too.
He also made exam 2 much easier than exam 1 and gave much better practice material, so most of these reviews are complaining about an un-curved, difficult, ill-prepared exam you won't get.
About the class itself, the homework is quite difficult, much much harder than the exams or anything else you will see in class except a few examples.
Professor Zheng gives out a lot of bonus points on the homework however, which makes getting a 100% in the homework category quite easy.
There are weekly quizzes in discussion written by the TA's, but these are very easy compared to everything else.
The review guides are pretty comprehensive and covered everything on exam 2.
Overall, STAT400 is a difficult class but Professor Zheng did a good job (in the second half of the semester) of teaching and preparing you for the exams.

Professor Richard, who looks slightly vicious, is a lovely person who is highly knowledgeable in optimization, linear algebra, calculus, and probability theory.
You may sometimes see him telling jokes with no expression during the lecture, and he is very patient with every student when you ask questions during office hours, class breaks, and class.
However, I admit that some course concepts are hard to comprehend at first glance.
Still, after reviewing the in-class material and combining it with the external video associated with that particular concept, the difficulty of the homework should be acceptable.
As for the midterm and final, he will provide some review material and the review section.
If you follow the pattern of the review materials, combined with some additional practice in linear algebra, both exams are good to go.
Furthermore, for the final project, I recommend consolidating the topic and working on the solution as soon as possible; it may take longer than you expected.
Professor Richard has the best mathematic expertise I have ever encountered at the University of Maryland.

As the final reminder, he will review the concept of linear algebra in optimization during the first several lectures; if you are not familiar with the relative concept, please try to catch it, which will make the rest of the lecture manageable.

really nice dude grading wise (replaced our final with our project grade), all material is pretty high-level and he doesn't ask anything outside of whats in the slides.
